senses.definition: having lungs or lung-like breathing organs.
examples: Some animals are called pulmonated because they breathe with lungs.
Pulmonated snails can live on land because they have lung-like organs.

senses.definition: a group of animals (especially some mollusks) that breathe with lungs or lung-like organs.
examples: Pulmonates include many land snails and slugs.
Scientists study how pulmonates breathe on land and in water.

senses.definition: plural of pulmonectomy: operations that remove all or part of a lung.
examples: Pulmonectomies are rare procedures performed for serious lung disease.
After pulmonectomies, patients need long-term follow-up.

senses.definition: a surgery that removes all or part of a lung.
examples: The doctor recommended a pulmonectomy after the tumor was found.
Recovery after a pulmonectomy can take several months.

senses.definition: a term meaning lungs, used especially in medical or anatomical contexts.
examples: In anatomy texts, pulmones may be used to refer to the lungs.
Damage to the pulmones can affect breathing.

senses.definition: connected with the lungs or with breathing.
examples: Pulmonic symptoms often include cough and shortness of breath.
The pulmonic circulation moves blood through the lungs.

senses.definition: connected with the lungs; lung-related.
examples: The report mentioned pulmonical issues that affected breathing.
Pulmonical research focuses on lung health and diseases.

senses.definition: the study or treatment of the lungs.
examples: Pulmonics includes diagnoses of lung infections and breathing problems.
Her work focuses on pulmonics and respiratory health.

senses.definition: an organism (often a mollusk) that has lung-like breathing organs.
examples: In biology texts, a pulmonifer is described as a lung-breather.
Pulmonifers can survive on land when the air is humid.

senses.definition: a name used in biological classification for lung-bearing organisms (especially certain snails and slugs).
examples: Pulmonifera is used as a group name in some taxonomy discussions.
Researchers compare pulmonifera species in different habitats.

senses.definition: having lungs or lung-like organs for breathing.
examples: Pulmoniferous animals breathe with organs like lungs.
Many pulmoniferous snails live in damp places.

senses.definition: inflammation of the lungs.
examples: The doctor diagnosed pulmonitis after the patient developed a persistent cough.
Pulmonitis can cause difficulty breathing and fever.

senses.definition: a doctor who specializes in lung diseases and breathing problems.
examples: She saw a pulmonologist for her asthma symptoms.
The pulmonologist ordered tests to check her lung function.

senses.definition: plural of pulmonologist: doctors who specialize in lung diseases and breathing problems.
examples: The hospital employs pulmonologists with different specialties.
Several pulmonologists reviewed her case.

senses.definition: an older medical device that helped people breathe, especially by mechanically pumping air into the lungs.
examples: In older records, a pulmotor was used to restart breathing.
A pulmotor can be found described in historical first-aid equipment.

senses.definition: plural of pulmotor: multiple older breathing-assistance devices.
examples: The museum displays pulmotors from the early 1900s.
Hospitals kept pulmotors as emergency equipment in the past.

senses.definition: connected with both the lungs and the trachea (windpipe).
examples: The scan showed a pulmotrachéal connection related to breathing.
A pulmotacheal route can be mentioned in anatomy descriptions.

senses.definition: a rare or technical term used in medicine to describe a condition or area involving the lungs and the trachea.
examples: Some specialized medical literature mentions pulmotrachearia.
The term pulmotrachearia appears in discussions of complex airway anatomy.

senses.definition: relating to the lungs and the trachea (windpipe).
examples: The clinician noted pulmotrachéary involvement in the airway area.
The report used a pulmotrachéary description for a specific pathway.

senses.definition: connected with the lungs and the trachea (windpipe).
examples: A pulmotrachéate structure is described in specialized anatomical notes.
The pulmotrachéate passage was discussed in the breathing-related section.

senses.definition: the soft, wet substance inside fruit or vegetables.
examples: The pulp of the orange is sweet and juicy.
Mash the pulp and mix it with sugar.

senses.definition: soft material made from crushed plant fibers, used to make paper.
examples: The paper is made from wood pulp.
Factories turn trees into pulp for newspapers.

senses.definition: a type of cheap magazine story or magazine, often with sensational content.
examples: In the past, pulp magazines were popular with readers.
Many old pulp stories were printed on low-quality paper.

senses.definition: soft like a pulp; pulpy.
examples: The plant leaves are pulpaceous and feel mushy.
After cooking, the fruit becomes pulpaceous.

senses.definition: connected with the pulp inside a tooth (the soft tissue in the center).
examples: She had pulpal pain that got worse at night.
A pulpal infection can sometimes spread to other areas of the mouth.

senses.definition: tooth pain caused by irritation or disease of the tooth pulp.
examples: The dentist explained that pulpalgia is pain coming from the pulp.
Cold and hot drinks triggered her pulpalgia.

senses.definition: in a way that relates to tooth pulp pain or tooth pulp inflammation.
examples: The pain pulpalley worsened after she chewed.
Symptoms became pulpalley, suggesting pulp irritation.
